content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Every AI project has a dirty secret.
The AI part is small. The un-sexy data work around it is huge.
So we did what business always does when it needs to sell something boring. We rebranded it.
“Data cleaning” is out. “Context engineering” is in.
Same work. Different LinkedIn title.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Rebrand
Then:
“Data cleaning is 80% of the work.”
Job title: Data Analyst. Budget: begrudging. Vibe: boring, thankless, but necessary.
Now:
“Context engineering is where the value is created.”
Job title: AI Context Engineer. Budget: enthusiastically funded. Vibe: cutting-edge, essential, worth every dollar.
Look closer. It’s the same job.
What “Context Engineering” Actually Means
If you strip away the AI-era terminology, context engineering is 4 things:
1. Making sure your data is clean (this is data cleaning)
2. Documenting what your data actually means (this is writing a data dictionary)
3. Defining your business rules (this is business analysis)
4. Keeping the definitions up to date as the business changes (this is data governance)
Are any of those new? No.
Have they gotten dramatically more important? Yes.
Have they gotten a cool new name so they get budget? Also yes.
Why the Rebrand Matters (Even If It’s Silly)
Here’s the honest reason I’m OK with it:
Data cleaning was chronically underfunded. Everyone knew it mattered. Nobody wanted to pay for it.
It was the intern’s job. The offshore team’s job. The thing you’d get to “when we have time” (never).
Context engineering has budget. Because AI is expensive. And AI-with-bad-context is embarrassingly expensive.
So companies are finally spending real money on the work that always mattered.
Same job. Same skills. Same tedious hours making sure the data actually means what people think it means.
But now it has stakeholders. And that’s a real upgrade.
Why AI Can’t Just Do This For You
There’s an obvious question: “Can’t AI clean my data?”
Not really. And this is the part most people miss.
AI is fine at some of the mechanical stuff. It can normalize date formats. It can spot obvious typos. It can flag rows that look weird.
But the hard part of “data cleaning” isn’t mechanical. It’s decisions.
- Which of these two “John Smith” rows is the same customer? (AI has to guess. You know.)
- Is the outlier in row 4,832 a real data point or a bug in our export? (AI has to guess. You know.)
- Should we count refunded orders as revenue? (AI has to guess. You know.)
Every one of those decisions requires business context. And business context lives in your team’s head, not in the data.
That’s the part AI can’t clean. And that’s the part context engineering exists to formalize.
What to Actually Do
If you’re an operator, analyst, or business person

• Notice when your team is doing “context engineering”

• Fund it. Budget for it. Give it real people.

• Write it down. The context that lives in people’s heads becomes context that lives in a shared document.
If you are an engineer or data person

• Focus on business context. Have more meetings, less coding.

• Document, document, document

• Log all data being used by AI processes at all times
The Take-Home
The most important work in AI is the un-sexy work under the AI.
For 30 years we called it data cleaning. Now we call it context engineering.
Same job. Better title.
